description_long: This object is a tall, monolithic bollard with a classic obelisk shape, featuring a four-sided tapering shaft that culminates in a pointed top. The surface appears to be made of weathered stone, with subtle textural variations and a matte finish, suggesting durability and resistance to outdoor elements. The base is broad and stepped, providing stability and a sense of permanence, typical of barriers used to control vehicle or pedestrian access in urban environments. One side of the bollard features a recessed rectangular niche, adding a unique architectural detail that distinguishes it from standard cylindrical or post-style bollards. The coloration is a consistent earthy brown, reminiscent of aged granite or concrete, with no visible reflective markings or decorative embellishments. This bollard is designed to be a fixed, immovable obstacle, likely embedded into the ground for maximum security.
description: A tall, obelisk-shaped stone bollard with a stepped base and a recessed niche on one side, finished in an earthy brown matte texture.
description_view_0: Front view shows the pointed obelisk shape and broad, stepped base with a smooth, uninterrupted surface.
description_view_1: Side view reveals the same obelisk profile, emphasizing the symmetry and solid construction.
description_view_2: Rear view highlights a deep, rectangular recessed niche running vertically up the shaft, distinguishing this side from the others.
description_view_3: Opposite side view mirrors the front, with a plain, uninterrupted obelisk face and the same stepped base.
